---
title: "Syllabus"
author: "Prof. Barker"
date: today
---

# Course Information {.unnumbered}

|                   |                          |
|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Course**        | Macroeconomics           |
| **Course Number** | ECON-XXXX                |
| **Semester**      | Fall 2025                |
| **Meeting Time**  | MWF 00:00 AM – 00:00 AM  |
| **Location**      | Building, Room XXX       |
| **Office Hours**  | By appointment           |
| **Email**         | yourname\@university.edu |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Course Description {.unnumbered}

This course provides a comprehensive introduction to macroeconomic theory and policy. Students will examine how economies function at the aggregate level, including the determinants of national output, employment, inflation, and economic growth. Special emphasis is placed on the role of fiscal and monetary policy in stabilizing the economy, as well as the linkages between domestic and international economies.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Learning Objectives {.unnumbered}

By the end of this course, students will be able to:

1.  Measure and interpret key macroeconomic indicators (GDP, CPI, unemployment)
2.  Apply classical and Keynesian models to analyze short- and long-run economic outcomes
3.  Evaluate the effectiveness of fiscal and monetary policy tools
4.  Analyze the causes and consequences of business cycle fluctuations
5.  Understand the open economy and the role of exchange rates and trade
6.  Use R to visualize and analyze macroeconomic data

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Required Materials {.unnumbered}

-   **Textbook:** \[Title\], Author, Edition — available at the campus bookstore or online
-   **Course Website:** \[your GitHub Pages URL\]
-   **Software:** R and RStudio (free — installation instructions provided)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Grading {.unnumbered}

| Component               | Weight   |
|-------------------------|----------|
| Homework / Problem Sets | 20%      |
| Quizzes                 | 15%      |
| Midterm Exam 1          | 20%      |
| Midterm Exam 2          | 20%      |
| Final Exam              | 25%      |
| **Total**               | **100%** |

### Grading Scale {.unnumbered}

| Grade | Range     |
|-------|-----------|
| A     | 90 – 100% |
| B     | 80 – 89%  |
| C     | 70 – 79%  |
| D     | 60 – 69%  |
| F     | Below 60% |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Course Schedule {.unnumbered}

*Note: Schedule is subject to change. Updates will be posted to the course website.*

| Week   | Topic                                                | Reading       |
|--------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------|
| 1      | Introduction to Macroeconomics                       | Ch. 1         |
| 2      | Measuring the Macroeconomy: GDP                      | Ch. 2         |
| 3      | Measuring the Macroeconomy: Inflation & Unemployment | Ch. 3         |
| 4      | Long-Run Growth: The Solow Model                     | Ch. 4         |
| 5      | Money, Banking, and Inflation                        | Ch. 5         |
| 6      | **Midterm Exam 1**                                   | Chs. 1–5      |
| 7      | Aggregate Demand and Supply                          | Ch. 6         |
| 8      | The IS-LM Model                                      | Ch. 7         |
| 9      | Fiscal Policy                                        | Ch. 8         |
| 10     | Monetary Policy                                      | Ch. 9         |
| 11     | **Midterm Exam 2**                                   | Chs. 6–10     |
| 12     | Business Cycles                                      | Ch. 10        |
| 13     | Open Economy Macroeconomics                          | Ch. 11        |
| 14     | Exchange Rates and Trade                             | Ch. 12        |
| 15     | Current Issues in Macroeconomics                     | TBD           |
| 16     | Review / Final Exam Prep                             | All           |
| Finals | **Final Exam**                                       | Comprehensive |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Course Policies {.unnumbered}

### Attendance {.unnumbered}

Regular attendance is expected. You are responsible for all material covered in class, regardless of attendance. More than **three unexcused absences** may result in a grade penalty.

### Late Work {.unnumbered}

Assignments submitted late will be penalized **10% per day** unless prior arrangements have been made. No work will be accepted more than **five days** after the due date.

### Academic Integrity {.unnumbered}

All work submitted must be your own. Use of unauthorized materials, collaboration without permission, or any form of academic dishonesty will be handled in accordance with university policy. When in doubt, ask.

### Use of AI Tools {.unnumbered}

The use of AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude) must be disclosed. AI may be used for brainstorming or checking work, but submitted work must reflect your own understanding. If you use AI assistance, include a brief note describing how it was used.

### Accommodations {.unnumbered}

Students requiring academic accommodations should contact the university's Office of Accessibility Services and provide documentation to the instructor within the first two weeks of class.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# A Note from Prof. Barker {.unnumbered}

Macroeconomics is one of the most relevant and dynamic fields in all of economics. The concepts we study — growth, inflation, unemployment, policy — appear in the news every day and affect every person in the economy. My goal is to help you build not just technical knowledge, but the ability to think critically about economic events and policy decisions.

Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have questions, fall behind, or just want to talk through an idea. I'm here to help.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; margin-top: 2rem;"}
[← Back to Preface](index.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary}

[Chapter 1 →](chapters/Chapter01.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary}
:::
